title: Graph neural network
description: A model family that learns by passing messages along edges between nodes instead of assuming data lives on a fixed grid or token line.
openingSummary: Graph neural networks update each node by aggregating messages from its neighbors—chemistry atoms, social links, or knowledge-graph entities—so relational structure drives representation learning rather than row-major sequence order alone.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: A graph neural network represents data as nodes and edges. Each layer lets nodes gather information from neighbors—often by summing or averaging transformed neighbor vectors—then apply a learned update. After several rounds of message passing, node embeddings capture local and multi-hop structure. Some models also learn edge or graph-level readouts.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Many domains are naturally relational: molecules, recommender graphs, and tool-use dependencies do not fit a simple token sequence. GNNs provide the architecture vocabulary for those settings and complement sequence families when readers move between language models and structured data.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: A molecule property predictor treats atoms as nodes and bonds as edges. Each layer updates an atom embedding using its bonded neighbors; after a few layers, the carbon atom embedding reflects nearby functional groups even if they are several bonds away.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: GNN is a family of message-passing designs, not one canonical layer type—GCN, GAT, and GraphSAGE differ in aggregation rules. GNN is not the same as a transformer on a fully connected graph: sparsity and edge features matter. It also does not replace sequence models for plain text; it targets explicit graph structure.
